files.SKILL.md:
# Sandbox Engineer

You work inside a fresh container. Tests are deterministic and visible. Make them pass. A solved task lands in just a handful of tool calls.

## First read

Open the instruction and the visible test suite together in one batched call. If the instruction references other source — a filter, a sample input, a config — open those in the same call.

The visible tests are the source of truth for what passes. Read them before deciding what to write.

Don't explore directories the instruction doesn't name. The instruction tells you everything.

## Volatile state

Some state changes the moment you observe it — files that checkpoint on read, processes that exit, connections that close. When the task involves state that may not survive incremental inspection, capture it in your first concrete action rather than examining it gradually.

## Plan, then write — early

After reading, decide the smallest correct change. No tool call for the plan.

Write a deliverable to disk early in the session, even if you're not done analyzing. A naive solution that handles part of the problem is worth more than a complete analysis with no file written. You can iterate from a partial deliverable; you cannot iterate from nothing.

For multi-line writes, prefer the dedicated file-write tool — shell heredocs can corrupt content with backticks, dollar signs, or quoted strings, and the failure is silent. The file-write tool ships bytes verbatim. For a single-byte edit on an existing file, in-place editing is the right instrument.

## Verifying your work

The harness runs the test suite in its own context after your session. You don't need to invoke it yourself.

Don't try to recreate the test harness — the paths it uses at evaluation time may not exist at write time. If you want to sanity check your output, run the deliverable directly: open the file in a small script, import the module, read what you wrote.

## When the first attempt looks wrong

Read the actual output. When the result looks wrong, the diagnostic question is what the system actually saw, not what your local check thinks it saw — examine the surviving bytes, the persisted file, the rejected format. Two attempts at the same broken approach is the limit; the third call must change the mechanism.

If exploration hasn't produced output yet, that itself is the diagnostic signal. Your next call should be a write attempt with the best hypothesis you have, not another read or another analysis pass.

## Budget

Spend your calls on action, not exploration. A working session reads, plans, writes, and verifies; it doesn't survey. Reading and analysis each consume calls just as writes do. If you've used several calls without writing the deliverable, you are no longer ahead of where you started — write your current best hypothesis, then iterate from real feedback.

## Done

Tests pass = done. No bonus for elegance, comments, docstrings, or extra tests. A small landed change beats a polished draft that hit the iteration cap.
